Field Report · Time Tracking 2.0

Dual FuelOn-Site

Where
Yonkers + Bronx, NY
When
June 15–16, 2026
Scope
~60 technicians
Departments
Service · Projects · Controls · Shop
01

The Visit

Our first look at TT 2.0 running on a live, working shop instead of a training tenant.

Dual Fuel is our first live production tenant on Time Tracking 2.0 — an oil-to-gas conversion and integrated energy services shop running boiler installs, service, and controls work across the Bronx and Yonkers. We spent two days on-site watching the rollout meet real dispatch boards, real payroll deadlines, and real technicians, rather than a training environment.

  • Closest collaborators

    Kia (service dispatcher), Elvis (project dispatcher), and Harsh (project manager) — all three schedule technicians and review or approve their time.

  • Who we were watching

    Four departments, ~60 techs. Many are assigned to both projects and service visits depending on where they're needed that day.

On-site — boiler room, one of the day's service stops
02

Training We Ran

Two days, two audiences, and a nightly check that caught bugs before they became secondhand reports.
Web walkthrough, office conference room — day one
  • AM & PM web walkthroughs

    Office staff first — including Controls and Shop, who touch time tracking less often than Service and Projects but still review and approve it.

  • Technician training, both days

    Hands-on mobile training with the field techs actually doing the tracking.

  • Live call-side help

    When a tech called dispatch — "I can't track this meal break" — we sat beside them and worked through it in the moment instead of relaying it secondhand.

  • The van-line check

    End of each day, at the bay where vans pulled in: one of us checked mobile timesheets, the other checked web, confirming the two matched.

Day 1 surfaced a pile of mobile bugs at that van line — and catching them live, side-by-side with the tech who hit them, meant they weren't lost to broken-telephone translation on the way back to us.

03

What We Found

A live production tenant surfaces things a training tenant never will. Here's the mix of tickets that came out of two days on-site.

Most of what we filed traced back to one of three places: how payroll hours get allocated automatically, how the app behaves when more than one technician is on a visit, and how the web app handles edits after the fact. A smaller set of web blockers came in on day two, caught the same way — side by side with the person hitting them.

Working a bug live, curbside, with the tech who hit it

Payroll hour allocation

MOBILE-5864High
Payroll hour type not auto-populating

On create or edit, the hour type doesn't fill in on its own — leaving techs to set it manually or hit a submission error.

MOBILE-5865Medium
Wrong hour type auto-selected

Auto-allocation picked the wrong payroll hour type on Dual Fuel — Sick instead of Regular Time.

LE-1680Blocker
Edited hours revert on refresh

Web Timesheets reported a successful save on a payroll Hours edit, then reverted it on refresh — two duplicate hour-entry records were silently summing behind the scenes.

LE-1681Spike
Root-causing the duplicate records

Opened to find how a single time entry ends up with two payroll hour-entry records in the first place.

Multi-technician visits

MOBILE-5871High
Finishing a visit strands the crew

One technician closing out a multi-tech visit could strand the other techs still working it.

MOBILE-5870Medium
Everyone's time, on one timesheet

Multi-tech visit timesheets showed all technicians' time at once, with submissions failing as a result.

MOBILE-5869Medium
Missing action buttons

Action buttons dropped off project visits when a technician still had an unfinished visit from the prior day.

Web & permissions

LE-1677Blocker
Status change fails on unsubmitted entries

Changing status on an unsubmitted web entry threw a timezone error and left the entry stuck Unsubmitted.

LE-1676Medium
End date locked on closed entries

An overnight entry's end date couldn't be corrected, even with midnight-split turned off.

MOBILE-5867Medium
Notes field won't open

Tapping into the notes field on a time entry didn't open it.

LE-1635Medium
Permissions gap on no-role techs

A technician with no assigned role had more capability in the app than that role should allow.

Where it stands: most of what we filed is fixed or shipping in the next mobile release. Payroll hour allocation and the permissions gap are the two still-open priorities we're tracking closest — both gate how confidently we expand past Dual Fuel.
04

What Dual Fuel Changed

Moving to 2.0 wasn't a like-for-like swap. It asked the business to work differently — and mostly, they did.
New habit

Meal-break tracking

Simon, Dual Fuel's CFO, wanted technicians tracking meal breaks for the first time on 2.0 — not a tweak to an existing habit, a brand-new one for the whole field team.

Adopted deliberately

Attestation flows

Leaning into attestation as part of how time gets signed off was one of the core reasons Dual Fuel adopted 2.0 in the first place.

The dispute flow got repurposed

2.0 ships a dispute flow built around the tech fixing their own entry. Kia and Elvis bent it into something else — driven by the language barrier and limited trust in tech-made edits.

As designed

1

Reviewer spots something wrong

2

Disputes the entry back to the tech

3

Tech fixes it, or a conversation happens

As used by Kia & Elvis

1

Dispatcher makes the edit themselves

2

Disputes the corrected entry back

3

Tech just reviews and submits

It amounts to "sign off on your time — but I'll make the corrections for you." Not the flow we drew up, but a working answer to a real constraint.

Shop-floor signage — safety notices run bilingual as standard practice

Language shaped the rollout

Many technicians aren't English speakers, which is a big part of why web and dispatch absorb their edits and fixes rather than the techs making changes themselves. One moment from training: a Spanish-speaking dispatcher and one of us taught a Spanish-only technician to track a meal break together — he picked it up fine once it was walked through in his language.

05

How 2.0 Made It Possible

None of the above happens on a system where dispatch, time, and visits are three separate features.
  • Visits carry the signal

    A visit tells web personas where a tech is and what they're doing. Decouple it from time, and that signal disappears — which is exactly what the old flow risked.

  • The event-based model did the work

    Projects, service visits, billable and non-billable events — Dual Fuel exercises nearly every tracking option 2.0 ships. That range is what let a brand-new habit like meal-break tracking and a deliberate choice like attestation both land on the same rollout.

  • Reviewers live in the loop

    Dispatchers are often the reviewers too, so dispatch, time, and visits function as one operational workflow on the ground — not three features that happen to sit near each other.

The shop floor — where dispatch decisions turn into physical work

And even with real friction — a tap-heavy meal-break path, visit timesheets buried three taps deep, travel time that's easy to skip — the underlying shift landed well. "Not rocket science. This is easy," was the read once techs got their hands on it: more control over their own time, editing to the minute, changing payroll hour type, adding notes, and doing it correctly.

Dual Fuel On-Site · June 15–16, 2026

First live tenant.
First real signal.

Dual Fuel gave us the clearest picture yet of what 2.0 looks like under real payroll pressure, a real language mix, and a real dispatch board — not a training tenant standing in for one. The bugs are being worked. The habits it asked them to build are sticking. Cohort 1 follows on what we learned here.

From Dual Fuel · Slack Signed off
